64	# Blueprint Writer
65	
66	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
67	
68	## Your Job
69	
70	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
71	
72	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Blueprint Writer Directive
78

80	<slug>
81	
82	## Target chapter
83	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
84

86	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
87	
88	## Required content
89	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
90	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
91	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
92	- ...
93	
94	## Out of scope
95	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
96	
97	## References
98	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
99	- ...
100	
101	## Expected outcome
102	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
103	```
104	
105	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
106	
107	## Chapter format
108	
109	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
110	
111	```latex
112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
115	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
116	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
119	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
121	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
122	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
123	\end{theorem}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
126	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
127	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
128	\begin{proof}
129	  \uses{thm:another_result}
130	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
131	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
132	\end{proof}
133	```
134	
135	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149	
150	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
157	
158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
159	
160	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.
167	
168	## Rules
169	
170	### What you CAN do
171	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
173	- **Add, correct, or complete `\uses{...}` cross-references** so the dependency edges match the real mathematics. If your chapter's proof of `T` invokes lemma `L`, `T` must declare `\uses{lem:L}`; if a `\uses{}` points at a label that no longer exists, fix it. Verify with `leandag` (see "Verifying dependencies with leandag" below) — do not eyeball it.

"It's trivial" is not a reason to skip it: the entry is what carries the dependency edges and what helps a later prover fill the sorry. When the helper's Lean proof relies on a fact with no blueprint entry, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" (or create it too, if it belongs in your chapter).

202	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.

204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
297	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.
300	
301	## Logging
302	
303	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-writer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).

320	- **Added theorem** `\theorem`/`\label{thm:foo}` — <one line on statement>
321	  - Proof sketch added: <Y/N + brief shape>

323	- **Fixed dependencies** `<label>` — corrected/added `\uses{...}` (was isolated / had a broken ref per leandag).
324	- **Removed** `<label>` — orphaned isolated block, removal authorized by directive.
325	- ...
326	
327	## Cross-references introduced

The plan agent and the reviewer use this list to verify that every `% SOURCE:` parenthetical points at a file you actually read. Omit this section only when you wrote zero citation blocks (Archon-original chapter).>
333	- `references/hartshorne-III-5.md` — verbatim quote for `\thm:smooth_criterion` (statement + proof).
334	- `references/stacks-tag-01V4.md` — verbatim quote for `\def:etale_morphism`.

2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).

81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. 84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.

152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.

1	\chapter{The Grassmannian over $\mathbb{Z}$: affine charts and gluing}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianCells}
3	
4	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
5	\label{sec:gr_setup}
6	
7	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
8	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
9	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
10	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
12	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
13	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
14	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
15	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
16	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).
17	
18	\begin{definition}
19	
20	\leanok
21	[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
22	  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}
23	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.affineChart}
24	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
25	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
26	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
27	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
28	  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
29	  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
30	  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
31	  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
32	  %  $I$.
33	  %
34	  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
35	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
36	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
37	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
38	  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
39	  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically
40	  %  isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$."
41	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
42	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
43	  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
44	  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
45	  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
46	  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
47	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
48	  set
49	  \[
50	    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
51	  \]
52	  The choice of an ordering of the free entries gives a (non-canonical)
53	  isomorphism \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\); in particular
54	  \(U^I\) is a smooth affine \(\mathbb{Z}\)-scheme of relative dimension
55	  \(d(r-d)\). Intuitively a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-point of \(U^I\) is a \(d \times r\)
56	  matrix whose \(I\)-columns form the identity, i.e.\ a rank-\(d\) quotient of
57	  \(\mathbb{Z}^{r}\) trivialised on the coordinates \(I\).
58	\end{definition}

60	\section{Transition maps and the cocycle condition}
61	\label{sec:gr_transition}
62	
63	\subsection*{Mathlib inputs for the transition map}
64	
65	The construction of \(\theta_{I,J}\) is a Cramer-inverse computation over the
66	localised chart ring, followed by one application of the universal property of a
67	localisation. We record the four Mathlib facts it rests on as dependency
68	anchors.

113	\subsection*{The transition map by matrix algebra}
114	
115	We now build \(\theta_{I,J}\) in seven steps over the localised chart ring
116	\(R^I_J := \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\), splitting at the matrix-algebra seams. The
117	ambient chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) is the polynomial ring of
118	\cref{def:gr_affine_chart} in the \(d(r-d)\) free variables \(x^I_{p,q}\)
119	(\(p \in \{1,\dots,d\}\), \(q \notin I\)).

376	  The pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre})
377	  sends \(P^J_I\) (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}, the \(I\)-minor determinant of
378	  \(X^J\)) to a unit of \(R^I_J\). Indeed
379	  \[
380	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I\bigr)
381	      = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1} = 1/P^I_J,
382	  \]
383	  which is a unit.
384	\end{lemma}
385	\begin{proof}
386	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
387	  \leanok
388	  By \cref{def:gr_transition_pre}, \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = M = (X^I_J)^{-1}
389	  X^I\), so \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) carries the \(I\)-minor \(X^J_I\) of \(X^J\) to
390	  the \(I\)-minor of \(M\), which is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) since
391	  the \(I\)-minor of \(X^I\) is the identity. Hence \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) =
392	  \det((X^I_J)^{-1}) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1}\). As \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit
393	  (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}), so is its inverse; the matrix-inverse
394	  determinant identity uses \cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}.
395	\end{proof}

397	\begin{definition}
398	
399	\leanok
400	[Transition map \(\theta_{I,J}\)]
401	  \label{def:gr_transition}
402	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap}
403	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, lem:gr_transition_pre_unit, lem:mathlib_away_lift, def:gr_affine_chart}
404	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
405	  % affine patches" (read from
406	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
407	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
408	  % "For any $I$ and $J$, a ring homomorphism
409	  %  $\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
410	  %  defined as follows. ...
411	  %  In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
412	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
413	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). The away-localisation lift of the
414	  %   pre-hom along `P^J_I`:
415	  %   `transitionMap d r I J :`
416	  %   `Localization.Away (minorDet d r J I) →+* Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J)`
417	  %   `:= IsLocalization.Away.lift (minorDet d r J I)`
418	  %   `(isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet d r I J)` applied to
419	  %   `(transitionPreMap d r I J : R^J →+* R^I_J)`.
420	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
421	  Because the pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to
422	  R^I_J\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) sends \(P^J_I\) to a unit of \(R^I_J\)
423	  (\cref{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}), the universal property of the
424	  away-localisation (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) provides a unique ring
425	  homomorphism extending it along \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I]\).
426	  This extension is the \emph{transition map}
427	  \[
428	    \theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \longrightarrow
429	    \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J], \qquad \theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I .
430	  \]
431	  Dually \(\theta_{I,J}\) is the comorphism of a morphism of schemes
432	  \(U^I_J \to U^J_I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), which we also denote
433	  \(\theta_{I,J}\); it sends the universal matrix \(X^I\) to \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\).
434	\end{definition}

436	\begin{lemma}
437	
438	\leanok
439	[\(\theta_{I,I}\) is the identity]
440	  \label{lem:gr_transition_self}
441	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap_self}
442	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
443	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
444	  % affine patches" (read from
445	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L838-L848).
446	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
447	  % "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$".
448	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
449	  %   `transitionMap_self d r I : transitionMap d r I I = RingHom.id _`
450	  %   (up to the identification `R^I_I = R^I` from `P^I_I = 1`).
451	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
452	  For every \(I\), \(\theta_{I,I} = \id_{U^I}\): since \(P^I_I = 1\)
453	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) the minor \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\) is its own
454	  inverse, so \(\theta_{I,I}(X^I) = (X^I_I)^{-1} X^I = X^I\), i.e.\
455	  \(\theta_{I,I}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity ring homomorphism
456	  of \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_I] = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\).
457	\end{lemma}
458	\begin{proof}
459	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
460	  \leanok
461	  When \(J = I\) one has \(P^I_I = \det(1_{d\times d}) = 1\)
462	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}), so \(R^I_I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\), the minor \(X^I_I\)
463	  is the identity, and its Cramer inverse is again the identity. Hence the image
464	  matrix is \(M = 1 \cdot X^I = X^I\), the pre-hom sends each \(x^I_{p,q}\) to
465	  itself, and its localisation lift (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity.
466	\end{proof}

468	\subsection*{Project-local matrix and minor bookkeeping}
469	
470	The cocycle computation rests on a handful of elementary matrix-algebra
471	identities over the (localised) chart rings. They are project-internal helpers
472	with no external source; each is verified directly in Lean.
473	
474	\begin{lemma}
475	
476	\leanok
477	[Column submatrix of a product]
478	  \label{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}
479	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.mul_submatrix_col}
480	  Let \(A \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(R)\) and
481	  \(B \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(R)\) be matrices over a commutative ring,
482	  and \(g : \{1,\dots,d\} \to \{1,\dots,r\}\) a column-index map. Then taking the
483	  column submatrix of the product equals multiplying by the column submatrix of
484	  the right factor:
485	  \[
486	    (A B)_{\bullet\, g} \;=\; A \,\bigl(B_{\bullet\, g}\bigr).
487	  \]
488	\end{lemma}
489	\begin{proof}
490	
491	\leanok
492	  Entrywise from the definition of matrix multiplication (reindexing the rows by
493	  the identity leaves the contraction index untouched). Proved directly in Lean.
494	\end{proof}
495	
496	\begin{lemma}
497	
498	\leanok
499	[A ring map commutes with the Cramer inverse]
500	  \label{lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}
501	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.map_nonsing_inv}
502	  \uses{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
503	  Let \(f : R \to S\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and
504	  \(A \in \operatorname{Mat}_{n\times n}(R)\) a square matrix with \(\det A\) a
505	  unit. Writing \(f_\ast\) for the entrywise application of \(f\), the
506	  nonsingular inverse is natural:
507	  \[
508	    \bigl(f_\ast A\bigr)^{-1} \;=\; f_\ast\bigl(A^{-1}\bigr).
509	  \]
510	\end{lemma}
511	\begin{proof}
512	
513	\leanok
514	  Since \(f_\ast A \cdot f_\ast(A^{-1}) = f_\ast(A A^{-1}) = f_\ast(1_{n\times n})
515	  = 1_{n\times n}\) (using \(A A^{-1} = 1\), \cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}),
516	  the matrix \(f_\ast(A^{-1})\) is a right inverse of \(f_\ast A\), hence equals
517	  \((f_\ast A)^{-1}\). Proved directly in Lean.
518	\end{proof}
519	
520	\begin{lemma}
521	
522	\leanok
523	[Functoriality of entrywise application]
524	  \label{lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp}
525	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.map_map_eq_of_comp}
526	  Let \(f : R \to A\), \(g : A \to D\) and \(h : R \to D\) be ring
527	  homomorphisms with \(g \circ f = h\). Then for any matrix \(M\) over \(R\),
528	  applying \(f\) then \(g\) entrywise equals applying \(h\) entrywise:
529	  \(g_\ast(f_\ast M) = h_\ast M\).
530	\end{lemma}
531	\begin{proof}
532	
533	\leanok
534	  Immediate from functoriality of \(\operatorname{Mat}(-)\) (composition of
535	  entrywise maps). Proved directly in Lean.
536	\end{proof}
537	
538	\begin{lemma}
539	
540	\leanok
541	[Inverse cancellation in a composite]
542	  \label{lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}
543	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}
544	  \uses{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
545	  Let \(A, B \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(R)\) and
546	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times e}(R)\) over a commutative ring, with
547	  \(\det A\) a unit. Then
548	  \[
549	    \bigl(B^{-1} A\bigr)\,\bigl(A^{-1} M\bigr) \;=\; B^{-1} M.
550	  \]
551	\end{lemma}
552	\begin{proof}
553	
554	\leanok
555	  By associativity and \(A A^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\)
556	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}). Proved directly in Lean.
557	\end{proof}
558	
559	\begin{lemma}
560	
561	\leanok
562	[The \(I\)-minor of \(X^I\) is the identity]
563	  \label{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
564	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
565	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix}
566	  The \(I\)-minor of the universal matrix \(X^I\)
567	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), with its \(I\)-columns read through the order
568	  isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \xrightarrow{\sim} I\), is the \(d\times d\)
569	  identity: \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\). (This is the normalisation \(P^I_I = 1\)
570	  defining the chart.)
571	\end{lemma}
572	\begin{proof}
573	
574	\leanok
575	  By \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix} the \(q\)-column with \(q \in I\) is the
576	  standard basis vector \(e_p\) where \(p\) is the preimage of \(q\) under the
577	  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \xrightarrow{\sim} I\); so the \(I\)-minor
578	  is the identity. Proved directly in Lean.
579	\end{proof}
580	
581	\begin{lemma}
582	
583	\leanok
584	[The \(J\)-minor of the image matrix is the identity]
585	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}

587	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}

590	  \[
591	    M_J \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_J \;=\; 1_{d\times d}.
592	  \]
593	\end{lemma}
594	\begin{proof}
595	
596	\leanok
597	  The \(J\)-column submatrix of \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is
598	  \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) times the \(J\)-column submatrix of \(X^I\), i.e.
599	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_J\), by \cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}; this is
600	  \(1_{d\times d}\) by the left-inverse identity
601	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}). Proved directly in Lean.
602	\end{proof}
603	
604	\begin{lemma}
605	
606	\leanok
607	[The \(I\)-minor of the image matrix is the Cramer inverse]
608	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_I}
609	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_submatrix_I}
610	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
611	  The \(I\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)

622	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}). Proved directly in Lean.

625	\begin{lemma}
626	
627	\leanok
628	[\(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) realises the matrix formula]
629	  \label{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}

669	  \det\bigl((\tilde\theta_{I,J})_\ast X^J\bigr)_K\); apply

699	\subsection*{Triple-overlap rings and the localised transition maps}
700	
701	The cocycle condition \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) cannot
702	be stated as a naive composition of the landed transition maps of
703	\cref{def:gr_transition}: the codomain of \(\theta_{J,K}\) (the ring
704	\(R^J[1/P^J_K]\)) differs from the domain of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (the ring
705	\(R^J[1/P^J_I]\)). The identity therefore lives over the \emph{triple-overlap}
706	rings obtained by inverting both relevant minors in each chart ring:
707	\[
708	  S_K := R^K[1/(P^K_I P^K_J)], \quad
709	  S_J := R^J[1/(P^J_I P^J_K)], \quad
710	  S_I := R^I[1/(P^I_J P^I_K)].
711	\]
712	
713	\begin{definition}
714	
715	\leanok
716	[Inclusion of an away-localisation into a double one (left)]
717	  \label{def:gr_away_incl_left}
718	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclLeft}
719	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_lift, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
720	  For \(x, y \in R\), the canonical inclusion
721	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y} : R[1/x] \to R[1/(xy)]\) inverting the extra factor
722	  \(y\), defined as the away-localisation lift (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) of
723	  the structure map \(R \to R[1/(xy)]\) along \(x\) — well-defined because \(x\)
724	  is a unit of \(R[1/(xy)]\) (a factor of the unit \(xy\),
725	  \cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}).
726	\end{definition}

1042	\section{Scheme-level glue-data layer}
1043	\label{sec:gr_glue_layer}
1044	
1045	The transition data of \cref{sec:gr_transition} lives at the level of rings. To
1046	feed it to a scheme-gluing machine we must re-express each piece as a morphism in
1047	the category of schemes: the charts \(U^I\), the principal-open overlaps
1048	\(U^I_J\), their inclusions, and the transitions \(\theta_{I,J}\) all become
1049	schemes and scheme morphisms by applying the contravariant functor \(\Spec\).
1050	The lemmas of this section record those translations together with the two
1051	genuinely geometric facts needed to assemble a glue datum: that each inclusion
1052	\(U^I_J \hookrightarrow U^I\) is an open immersion, and that the fibre product of
1053	two principal opens of \(\Spec A\) is again a principal open. They are
1054	project-internal bridge results with no external source beyond the gluing
1055	construction of \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}.

1324	\section{The glued Grassmannian scheme}
1325	\label{sec:gr_glued}
1326	
1327	\begin{definition}
1328	[The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
1329	  \label{def:gr_glued_scheme}
1330	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scheme}
1331	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle,
1332	    def:gr_chart_overlap, def:gr_chart_incl, lem:gr_chartIncl_isOpenImmersion,
1333	    lem:gr_chartIncl_self_isIso, def:gr_chart_transition,
1334	    lem:gr_chartTransition_self, def:gr_away_pullback_iso,
1335	    lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_fst, lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_snd,
1336	    def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}
1337	  % NOTE (iter-028 formalization status): the glue-datum fields t' and t_fac ARE
1338	  % formalized (Lean `chartTransition'` / `chartTransition'_fac`, axiom-clean).
1339	  % The cocycle field, the `Scheme.GlueData` bundle, and `Grassmannian.scheme`
1340	  % itself are NOT yet formalized: the categorical reduction is solved (the
1341	  % pullback isomorphisms telescope away) and the residual is the rotated ring
1342	  % cocycle identity Phi = id, recorded in the end-of-file HANDOFF comment of
1343	  % GrassmannianCells.lean. The \lean{...AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scheme}
1344	  % target above therefore does not yet exist in Lean.
1345	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
1346	  % affine patches" (read from
1347	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L843-L850).
1348	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1349	  % "Therefore the schemes
1350	  %  $U^I$, as $I$ varies over all the
1351	  %  ${r\choose d}$ different
1352	  %  subsets of  $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of cardinality $d$,
1353	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$ to form a
1354	  %  finite-type scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$. As each $U^I$ is
1355	  %  isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
1356	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$."
1357	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1358	  Gluing the affine charts \(\{U^I\}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), one for each
1359	  of the \(\binom{r}{d}\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) of cardinality
1360	  \(d\), along the open subschemes \(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\) via the transition
1361	  isomorphisms \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I_J \xrightarrow{\sim} U^J_I\)
1362	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}) -- which satisfy the cocycle condition
1363	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\)
1364	  of \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} -- yields a scheme
1365	  \[
1366	    \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \longrightarrow \Spec \mathbb{Z}.
1367	  \]
1368	  It is of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\) (a finite gluing of finitely
1369	  generated \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras), and under the gluing each \(U^I\) is an
1370	  open subscheme of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J\).
1371	
1372	  \emph{Construction of the glue datum.} The gluing is carried out through the
1373	  Mathlib vehicle for scheme gluing, a glue datum in the sense of a category of
1374	  schemes (the structure \(\mathtt{Scheme.GlueData}\), extending the abstract
1375	  glue-datum interface). Its data are supplied as follows. The index set is the
1376	  set \(J := \{\, I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\} : \#(I) = d \,\}\) of size-\(d\)
1377	  subsets. The objects are the charts \(U^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}); the
1378	  pairwise overlaps are the schemes \(U^I_J\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_overlap}); the
1379	  inclusions \(f_{I,J} : U^I_J \to U^I\) are the open immersions
1380	  \(\cref{def:gr_chart_incl}\) (open immersions by
1381	  \cref{lem:gr_chartIncl_isOpenImmersion}, in particular monomorphisms), with the
1382	  identity field \(f_{\mathrm{id}}\) supplied by
1383	  \cref{lem:gr_chartIncl_self_isIso}; the transition isomorphisms
1384	  \(t_{I,J} : U^I_J \to U^J_I\) are the scheme-level transitions
1385	  \(\cref{def:gr_chart_transition}\), with identity field \(t_{\mathrm{id}}\)
1386	  supplied by \cref{lem:gr_chartTransition_self}.
1387	
1388	  The remaining datum is the triple-overlap cocycle field
1389	  \(t'\). On the relevant fibre products it is a morphism
1390	  \[
1391	    U^I_J \times_{U^I} U^I_K \longrightarrow U^J_K \times_{U^J} U^J_I ,
1392	  \]
1393	  built by transporting through the pullback identification of
1394	  \cref{def:gr_away_pullback_iso}: it is the composite of the pullback
1395	  isomorphism (\cref{def:gr_away_pullback_iso}) on the source, the \(\Spec\) of
1396	  the localised triple-overlap transition \(\Theta_{I,J}\)
1397	  (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}), the \(\Spec\) of the order-swap isomorphism
1398	  (\cref{def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}), and the inverse pullback isomorphism on the
1399	  target. The order-swap isomorphism is inserted precisely to reconcile the
1400	  opposite product orders \(A[1/(P^J_I P^J_K)]\) and \(A[1/(P^J_K P^J_I)]\)
1401	  carried by the source transition and the target pullback identification.
1402	
1403	  The two coherence obligations are then matrix-algebra identities pulled back to
1404	  rings. The compatibility field \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) (that \(t'\) is compatible
1405	  with the projections to the charts) reduces -- both fibre products being affine
1406	  -- to an identity of ring homomorphisms, obtained from the leg lemmas
1407	  \cref{lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_fst} and \cref{lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_snd}
1408	  together with the faithfulness of \(\Spec\) on affine schemes, after which the
1409	  identity is checked on generators by the universal property of the
1410	  away-localisation. The cocycle field
1411	  \(t'_{I,J,K} \circ t'_{J,K,I} \circ t'_{K,I,J} = \mathrm{id}\) reduces, after
1412	  the pullback isomorphisms telescope away, to exactly the ring cocycle condition
1413	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) of \cref{lem:gr_cocycle},
1414	  modulo the inserted order-swaps. The glued scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is then
1415	  the colimit \((\text{glue datum}).\mathrm{glued}\) produced by the gluing
1416	  machine.
1417	
1418	  Moreover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is \emph{smooth of relative
1419	  dimension \(d(r-d)\)}. Smoothness and relative dimension are local on the
1420	  source; the charts \(\{U^I\}\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and each
1421	  \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) is
1422	  affine space of dimension \(d(r-d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\), which is smooth over
1423	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\) (the polynomial ring
1424	  \(\mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_{d(r-d)}]\) is a smooth \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra). As
1425	  the property may be checked on an open cover of the source, the conclusion
1426	  follows.
1427	\end{definition}

1429	\section{Separatedness}
1430	\label{sec:gr_separated}
1431	
1432	\begin{lemma}
1433	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
1434	  \label{lem:gr_separated}
1435	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isSeparated}
1436	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
1437	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Separatedness" (read from
1438	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860).
1439	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1440	  % "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with $U^I\times U^J$
1441	  %  can be seen to be the closed subscheme $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$
1442	  %  defined by entries of the matrix formula $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$,
1443	  %  and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
1444	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1445	  The structure morphism \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
1446	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is separated; equivalently, the diagonal
1447	  \(\Delta : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \times_{\mathbb{Z}}
1448	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is a closed immersion.
1449	\end{lemma}
1450	
1451	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with
1452	% $U^I\times U^J$ can be seen to be the closed subscheme
1453	% $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
1454	% $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$, and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
1455	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860.)
1456	\begin{proof}
1457	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
1458	  The products \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), as \(I, J\) range over the
1459	  size-\(d\) subsets, form an affine open cover of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)
1460	  \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Separatedness may be checked on this
1461	  cover: it suffices to show that for each pair \((I,J)\) the preimage
1462	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J)\) maps by a closed immersion into
1463	  \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), i.e.\ that the corresponding ring
1464	  homomorphism is surjective.
1465	
1466	  By \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme} the diagonal preimage is the overlap
1467	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J) = U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J = \Spec
1468	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\). The restricted diagonal corresponds to the ring map
1469	  \[
1470	    \delta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]
1471	    \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J],
1472	    \qquad
1473	    X^I \otimes 1 \mapsto X^I, \quad 1 \otimes X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
1474	  \]
1475	  the second component being the comorphism \(\theta_{I,J}\) of
1476	  \cref{def:gr_transition} (a point of \(U^I_J\) and its image under
1477	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) are the two coordinates of a point of the diagonal). We claim
1478	  \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Its image contains all entries of \(X^I\)
1479	  (from the first factor). From the second factor it contains the entries of
1480	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\); taking the \(I\)-minor of this matrix, and using
1481	  \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\), the image contains the entries of \((X^I_J)^{-1}\),
1482	  and in particular
1483	  \[
1484	    \delta_{I,J}(1 \otimes P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = 1/P^I_J .
1485	  \]
1486	  Hence \(1/P^I_J\) lies in the image, so the image is all of
1487	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) and \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Concretely the
1488	  closed subscheme \(\Delta_{I,J} \subseteq U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\) is cut
1489	  out by the entries of the matrix relation
1490	  \[
1491	    X^J_I \, X^I - X^J \;=\; 0,
1492	  \]
1493	  which on the diagonal holds because \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) forces
1494	  \(X^J_I = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) and therefore \(X^J_I X^I =
1495	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I = X^J\). As each restricted diagonal is a closed immersion,
1496	  \(\Delta\) is a closed immersion and \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over
1497	  \(\mathbb{Z}\).
1498	\end{proof}

1562	\begin{proof}
1563	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated, def:gr_transition}
1564	  Since \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is of finite type
1565	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) and separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), and
1566	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) is Noetherian, properness follows from the valuative criterion
1567	  for discrete valuation rings: it suffices to show that every solid diagram
1568	  \[
1569	    \begin{array}{ccc}
1570	      \Spec K & \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } & \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \\
1571	      \downarrow & & \downarrow \pi \\
1572	      \Spec R & \longrightarrow & \Spec \mathbb{Z}
1573	    \end{array}
1574	  \]
1575	  with \(R\) a discrete valuation ring, \(K = \operatorname{Frac}(R)\) and
1576	  valuation \(\nu : K \to \mathbb{Z} \cup \{\infty\}\), admits a unique diagonal
1577	  filler \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
1578	
1579	  \emph{Existence.} The morphism \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) lands
1580	  in some chart \(U^I\) (\(\Spec K\) has one point), so it is given by a ring map
1581	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\). Among the \(\binom{r}{d}\) minors choose \(J\)
1582	  with \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) minimal. Because \(P^I_I = 1\) gives \(f(P^I_I) = 1\)
1583	  with \(\nu = 0\), the minimum satisfies \(\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0\); in particular
1584	  \(f(P^I_J) \ne 0\), so \(f(X^I_J) \in \mathrm{GL}_d(K)\) is invertible over
1585	  \(K\). Define
1586	  \[
1587	    g : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \longrightarrow K, \qquad
1588	    g(X^J) = f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\bigr) ;
1589	  \]
1590	  this is the composite of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) with
1591	  \(f\), so \(g\) defines the same \(K\)-point \(\varphi\), now exhibited as
1592	  landing in \(U^J\).
1593	
1594	  We show every entry \(g(x^J_{p,q})\) lies in \(R\), i.e.\ has \(\nu \ge 0\).
1595	  First, for any size-\(d\) subset \(K' \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) the \(K'\)-minor
1596	  of \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\), so
1597	  \[
1598	    g(P^J_{K'}) = \det f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\bigr)
1599	    = \frac{f(P^I_{K'})}{f(P^I_J)},
1600	    \qquad
1601	    \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) = \nu(f(P^I_{K'})) - \nu(f(P^I_J)) \ge 0
1602	  \]
1603	  by minimality of \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\). Now the \(J\)-minor \(X^J_J\) of \(X^J\) is
1604	  the identity, so by cofactor expansion each individual entry
1605	  \(x^J_{p,q}\) (with \(q \notin J\)) equals, up to sign, the minor
1606	  \(P^J_{K'}\) for \(K' = (J \setminus \{j_p\}) \cup \{q\}\) -- the \(d \times d\)
1607	  determinant obtained by replacing the \(p\)-th identity column of \(X^J_J\) by
1608	  the column \(q\). Hence \(\nu(g(x^J_{p,q})) = \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) \ge 0\) for all
1609	  entries, and the entries with \(q \in J\) are \(0\) or \(1\). Therefore
1610	  \(g(\mathbb{Z}[X^J]) \subseteq R\), so \(g\) factors uniquely as
1611	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R \hookrightarrow K\). The corresponding morphism
1612	  \(\Spec R \to U^J \hookrightarrow \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) restricts to \(\varphi\)
1613	  over \(\Spec K\) and lies over \(\Spec \mathbb{Z}\), providing the filler.
1614	
1615	  \emph{Uniqueness.} Any two fillers agree over the generic point \(\Spec K\)
1616	  and, \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) being separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)
1617	  (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), two morphisms \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
1618	  agreeing on the schematically dense open \(\Spec K\) coincide. This verifies
1619	  the valuative criterion, so \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is
1620	  proper.
1621	\end{proof}

1623	\section{Out of scope}
1624	\label{sec:gr_out_of_scope}
1625	
1626	This chapter constructs the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
1627	the absolute base \(\mathbb{Z}\) and establishes its smoothness, separatedness
1628	and properness. The following constructions are deferred to later chapters and
1629	are not treated here:
1630	\begin{itemize}
1631	  \item \textbf{The tautological/universal quotient} \(u : \mathcal{O}^{\oplus
1632	    r}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) and the determinant
1633	    line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\) with its Pl\"ucker embedding.
1634	  \item \textbf{The functor-of-points representability} of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
1635	    -- that it represents \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d) =
1636	    \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^r \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}} /
1637	    \mathbb{Z} / \mathbb{Z}}\); this is blueprinted as
1638	    \cref{thm:grassmannian_representable} in
1639	    \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}.
1640	  \item \textbf{The relative version over a general base \(S\)} with a locally
1641	    free sheaf \(V\) of rank \(r\): the scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V,d)\) obtained by
1642	    base change and gluing over a trivialising cover of \(V\). The
1643	    absolute-over-\(\mathbb{Z}\) construction of this chapter is the input to
1644	    that base change.
1645	\end{itemize}

\begin{lemma}
[The order-swap lies over the base ring]
  \label{lem:gr_awayMulCommEquiv_comp_algebraMap}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayMulCommEquiv_comp_algebraMap}
  \uses{def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv, lem:mathlib_isLocalization_algEquiv}
  The order-swap isomorphism (\cref{def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}) intertwines the
  two structure maps: for \(x, y \in A\),
  \[
    \mathrm{swap}_{x,y} \circ \bigl(A \to A[1/(xy)]\bigr)
      \;=\; \bigl(A \to A[1/(yx)]\bigr).
  \]
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv, lem:mathlib_isLocalization_algEquiv}
  Both \(A[1/(xy)]\) and \(A[1/(yx)]\) are localisations of \(A\) at the same
  submonoid (\(xy\) and \(yx\) generate it), and the canonical comparison map
  between two such localisations is an \(A\)-algebra isomorphism
  (\cref{lem:mathlib_isLocalization_algEquiv}); an \(A\)-algebra map commutes with
  the structure maps. Proved directly in Lean.
\end{proof}

\subsection*{The triple-overlap glue field \(t'\)}

The scheme-gluing machine requires, beyond the pairwise transitions \(t_{I,J}\), a
\emph{triple-overlap} field \(t'\) on the relevant fibre products, together with
its compatibility \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) with the chart projections. We record both
as the next layer of bridge results; their cocycle coherence is the residual
obligation discharged in \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}.

\begin{definition}
[The triple-overlap transition \(t'\)]
  \label{def:gr_chart_transition'}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartTransition'}
  \uses{def:gr_away_pullback_iso, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}
  The \(t'\)-field of the Grassmannian glue datum is the morphism of schemes
  \[
    t'_{I,J,K} : U^I_J \times_{U^I} U^I_K \longrightarrow U^J_K \times_{U^J} U^J_I ,
  \]
  defined as the composite
  \[
    U^I_J \times_{U^I} U^I_K
      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \Spec S_I
      \xrightarrow{\ \Spec \Theta_{I,J}\ } \Spec S_J
      \xrightarrow{\ \Spec \mathrm{swap}\ } \Spec R^J[1/(P^J_K P^J_I)]
      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } U^J_K \times_{U^J} U^J_I ,
  \]
  where the first and last arrows are the pullback identifications of
  \cref{def:gr_away_pullback_iso} -- the source one for the pair \((P^I_J, P^I_K)\)
  over \(R^I\), and the target one (taken inverse) for the pair \((P^J_K, P^J_I)\)
  over \(R^J\) -- the middle arrow is the \(\Spec\) of the localised
  triple-overlap transition \(\Theta_{I,J} : S_J \to S_I\)
  (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}), and \(\mathrm{swap}\) is the \(\Spec\) of the
  order-swap isomorphism \(R^J[1/(P^J_K P^J_I)] \cong S_J = R^J[1/(P^J_I P^J_K)]\)
  (\cref{def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}). The order-swap is inserted precisely to
  reconcile the product order \(P^J_K P^J_I\) carried by the target pullback
  identification with the order \(P^J_I P^J_K\) of the codomain \(S_J\) of
  \(\Theta_{I,J}\).
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}
[The \(t'\) ring identity]
  \label{lem:gr_chartTransition'_ringIdentity}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartTransition'_ringIdentity}
  \uses{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv, def:gr_away_incl_right, def:gr_away_incl_left, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_awayMulCommEquiv_comp_algebraMap, lem:mathlib_away_lift}
  Over the triple-overlap rings, the localised transition \(\Theta_{I,J}\)
  (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}) pre-composed with the order-swap and the right
  inclusion equals the left inclusion post-composed with the plain transition
  \(\theta_{I,J}\), as ring homomorphisms \(R^J[1/P^J_I] \to S_I\):
  \[
    \Theta_{I,J} \circ \mathrm{swap} \circ \iota^{\mathrm{R}}_{P^J_K, P^J_I}
      \;=\; \iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{P^I_J, P^I_K} \circ \theta_{I,J} .
  \]
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv, def:gr_away_incl_right, def:gr_away_incl_left, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_awayMulCommEquiv_comp_algebraMap, lem:mathlib_away_lift}
  Both sides are ring maps out of the away-localisation \(R^J[1/P^J_I]\), so by the
  universal property of the localisation (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) it suffices
  to check equality after pre-composing with the structure map
  \(R^J \to R^J[1/P^J_I]\), i.e.\ on the chart ring \(R^J\). There the right
  inclusion and the order-swap collapse to structure maps
  (\cref{lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap},
  \cref{lem:gr_awayMulCommEquiv_comp_algebraMap}), and the lift defining
  \(\Theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}) unwinds to
  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}} \circ \tilde\theta_{I,J}\)
  (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_left}); the right side likewise unwinds through the lift
  defining \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) to the same
  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}} \circ \tilde\theta_{I,J}\). Proved directly in Lean.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[The \(t'\) compatibility field \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)]

The triple-overlap field \(t'\) and its first coherence obligation are supplied
  by the two preceding results. The field itself is the morphism
  \(t'_{I,J,K} : U^I_J \times_{U^I} U^I_K \to U^J_K \times_{U^J} U^J_I\) of
  \cref{def:gr_chart_transition'}, and the compatibility field
  \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) -- that \(t'\) is compatible with the projections to the
  charts -- is \cref{lem:gr_chartTransition'_fac}, which reduces (both fibre
  products being affine) to the ring identity
  \cref{lem:gr_chartTransition'_ringIdentity} via the leg lemmas
  \cref{lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_fst} and \cref{lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_snd}.

  \emph{The residual cocycle obligation.} What remains is the \(t'\)-cocycle
  field, the coherence
  \[
    t'_{I,J,K} \;\circ\; t'_{J,K,I} \;\circ\; t'_{K,I,J} \;=\; \mathrm{id}
  \]
  on the source triple overlap. Its categorical reduction is already
  achieved: in the threefold composite, each of the two internal pairs of the
  form \(\mathrm{ap}_{X,Y}^{-1} \circ \mathrm{ap}_{X,Y}\) -- the inverse of one
  factor's target pullback identification against the next factor's source
  pullback identification (\cref{def:gr_away_pullback_iso}) -- cancels, the two
  identifications sharing the same instance because all three \(t'\)-factors are
  built from the same construction (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition'}). Stripping
  the outer conjugating pullback isomorphism (its two ends likewise cancel) and
  collapsing the six remaining \(\Spec\)-arrows into a single \(\Spec\) leaves a
  pure ring obligation: the identity of ring homomorphisms
  \[
    \Phi \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{\,R^I[1/(P^I_J P^I_K)]}, \qquad
    \Phi \;:=\; \Theta_{I,J} \circ \mathrm{swap}_J \circ \Theta_{J,K}
      \circ \mathrm{swap}_K \circ \Theta_{K,I} \circ \mathrm{swap}_I ,
  \]
  where \(\Theta_{X,Y}\) is the localised triple-overlap transition
  (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}, taken for the rotated index triple) and each
  \(\mathrm{swap}_X\) is the matching order-swap isomorphism
  (\cref{def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}). This is a rotated analogue of the ring
  cocycle condition \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}: it is proved by
  reducing -- via the universal property of the away-localisation, applied to the
  generators \(\mathrm{powers}(P^I_J P^I_K)\) -- to the chart-ring generators, and
  then reusing the image-matrix cocycle computation
  \cref{lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq} (the same \((Y_K)^{-1} Y\) collapse that
  underlies \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}). With this field in hand the glue datum is
  complete: it is the structure \(\mathtt{Scheme.GlueData}\) with
  \(U := \texttt{affineChart}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}),
  \(V := \texttt{chartOverlap}\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_overlap}),
  \(f := \texttt{chartIncl}\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_incl}),
  \(t := \texttt{chartTransition}\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition}),
  \(t_{\mathrm{id}} := \texttt{chartTransition\_self}\)
  (\cref{lem:gr_chartTransition_self}),
  \(t' := \texttt{chartTransition'}\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition'}),
  \(t_{\mathrm{fac}} := \texttt{chartTransition'\_fac}\)
  (\cref{lem:gr_chartTransition'_fac}), and the cocycle field above. The glued
  scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is then the colimit
  \((\text{glue datum}).\mathrm{glued}\) produced by the gluing machine.
